result:
He kept his pencils in a cup that used to be a candle. If he looked closely, he could see the faint pink wax on the inside wall, like the memory of a rose. The cup lived on the only clear corner of his drafting table, which was otherwise a topography of maps. Not maps of streets or rivers or borders, not the functional sort people fold in cars. He mapped the places that had vanished. He mapped rooms that had been rearranged so well the old arrangement could not even be guessed. He mapped cool spots on pillows after the sleeper sat up, and the spot of darkness where a tree’s shadow fell before the tree was cut down. He mapped the sound a ceramic sink used to make when the faucet dripped at 3 a.m. He sold these to people who came to him by rumor and by shrug: a neighbor’s cousin’s barber said there was a man in the city who could put your gap on paper.

His shop was up a thin staircase above a locksmith who specialized in keys to doors whose buildings no longer stood. Every morning, the locksmith swept dust into a pan as if he were tidying up silences. The cartographer would come down for coffee and stand with the locksmith in the threshold, watching the city’s light figure out what kind of day it wanted to be. They said little. There is a friendliness that springs up between those who deal in absences. They learn not to crowd them.

One late afternoon in late season, when the heat could be bitten into like fruit, a woman came in holding both hands around nothing. People often held their losses with both hands, as if they might fall or leak. She said her name was Mara and that she had lost a Sunday morning. Not a whole day, she corrected, the part from when the light came in through the maple leaves and made coins on the kitchen table to when the water on the stovetop began to whisper. It had been the kind of morning that slides into the cup of your week and makes the other days sweeter. Her father had been staying with her then, three years ago, and they had made pancakes, and he had wiped the skillet with the corner of a newspaper page, which she knew you weren’t supposed to do, and she had wanted to snap at him about transfats, but the sunlight had made a pattern on his wrist and she had swallowed the correction and said nothing. She could remember that she had tried not to cry at nothing. She could remember trying not to cry. But she could not find the morning itself. The years had rubbed over it. When she reached for it, her hands came up with a smooth spot, the way paint does when you stand a picture frame in the same place for a long time.

He asked her to tell him where the morning used to be kept. She described a round table with a scar near the place where the wood’s grain made an eye, a window that stuck in damp weather, a pot that hissed at the lid whether or not the heat was high. He took his satchel and his cup of faintly pink pencils and went to her apartment while the light drained down behind rooftops. Cartography done in the raw has to be done quickly; the city is always rearranging its absences.

He found the place where the morning had been. He crouched by the table and placed his ear close to the wood. A map does not begin with sight; it begins with an agreement to be wrong. He heard the soft chafe of newspaper, the kiss of batter against pan. He smelled a sweetness that was not sugar but heat meeting sugar, a browning. He let his fingertips walk the table’s ring that the cup made, and they tingled at a radius that remembered being warm. He spread paper and took out his inks: one that was tea made too strong, one that was milk gone warm, one that was water in which a copper coin had soaked. He drew the outline of the absence the way you might trace a shadow, lightly, as if not to wake it. Where the morning had been bright, he left the page unmarked. Where it had been quiet, he shaded with the side of the pencil, soft as lint. He marked the path of a small draft that had come from the window edge and made the pancake edges frill on one side more than the other. His hand knew where to put the first clearing, the first small nothing in the forest of the day.

On the back of the paper, which he never showed clients, he kept notes of the trails. A missing thing is rarely truly gone. It goes to where such things gather: the Museum of Skies Before Rain, the Basement Cupboard of Unasked Questions, the Municipal Warehouse for Screws That Drop with a Tink and Then Disappear. Mara’s Sunday had slid, as some do, into the market under the rail bridge where vendors sell time by the spoon. It was a clean place, that market, though it looked like it had been sketched into the world hastily. People came to barter away five minutes they didn’t want to live again in exchange for five they’d like to try differently. A man with a face like gravel had a stall of hours that fall between elevator floors. A woman trimmed with tassels inched threads of time through her fingers and knitted them into dishcloths you could only use once. He went at gray light when the sellers were setting out their hush.

He brought Mara’s map. He laid it on the vendor’s counter, facing up, and tilted it so the light would catch the milk lines just right. The vendor, who had an eye that twitched as if it were always trying to look around corners, admired the workmanship and said he could have traded this for a summer seventeen years ago. He wanted only one thing for the morning: a bright scrap of a thing that meant little and couldn’t be found twice. The cartographer had a small case for that. He flipped it open and considered. He could give the color of the rubber band that held his mother’s hair postcards together, but he had already traded that, long ago, for the ability to sleep on trains. He could give the word for the exact relief one feels when a pot doesn’t boil over—a dialect word from a coast he had once visited and muttered on the ferry. He could give the tune that always arrived when he smelled cut grass. He unhooked that tune from where it floated in him, and the vendor’s face softened. They both listened to the place it left, which hummed. It was a good trade. He paid the rest with a sock that had never had a partner.

The vendor boxed the Sunday like a fragile pie and warned, as they do, that refolded time holds a crease. The cartographer carried it in both hands through the morning shoppers who juggled days, whose arms were full of scenes. He felt the weight of it change with each street he crossed, as if streets had different gravities. When he put it down on Mara’s table, the scar in the wood aligned with a half-moon of metal on the box, the kind of fit that feels like fate and is mostly craft.

They let the morning out. It came like a smell first, then like a color that the room remembered how to wear. He saw the window blink and then stick, the way it had done, and he saw Mara put her hand to her mouth as if she might keep the past from fogging and running. He told her to sit where she had sat, and he handed her a wooden spoon to hold just to weight her, just to tell her hand what to do. The pancakes did not arrive; this was a map, not an orchard. Instead, something leaned in from a place where her father still stood near a stove. The sound of his breath on a hot morning brushed the side of their faces. Mara laughed and then cried into her wrist. The light coin-spattered the table. It was not perfect. The seam showed, if one knew where to look. The milk in the ink had browned slightly where it had dried too long. But it lay over the day, as a well-mended shirt does over a shoulder, warm and enough.

After she left with the map folded into her coat, he washed his cup and found that he could not hum. He tried, and nothing rose. He went to the window to call to the locksmith about nothing in particular and found that he could not remember, for a thudding second, the slid slant of his mother’s neat italic. He could remember that it was neat and italic, but not the exact lean of it, not the stubbornness in the letter f. He stood very still with that absence, named it without writing it down, and let it be. We are full of barter.

On his wall, pinned with brass tacks that made soft holes in the plaster, were maps of evaporations: a path someone’s dog used to make in the grass; the zone of warmth under a reading lamp; the square inside a refrigerator just before the light swallows dark. As the afternoon drew itself thin, a new empty spot appeared on the corner of his table, a neat oval like a thumb pressed into dough. He knew it at once: the weight of a hand that had learned to hold and release. He sharpened his pencil until it sang and drew a shape that left a place for light to fall. The city breathed, misplacing and remembering, and he leaned into the tender labor of keeping track of what kindness leaves when it passes through.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
